one_line_takeaway: RedSage shows that an 8B open model, built from an 11.7B-token filtered cyber corpus plus 266K agentically-augmented SFT dialogues on Qwen3-8B-Base, can beat comparable 8B (and even 32B) baselines on cybersecurity and general benchmarks while remaining locally deployable, though it still trails GPT-5 and its self-generated benchmark warrants caution.
key_finding: At 8B scale, domain-aware CPT + agentic SFT + DPO improves cybersecurity performance without the general-capability degradation seen in prior cyber-tuned models. On RedSage-Bench MCQ, RedSage-8B-Ins reaches 85.73 macro-accuracy vs Qwen3-8B's 81.85 (+3.88) and even edges out Qwen3-32B (85.40). On established cybersecurity benchmarks (Tab 5 mean), the instruct RedSage beats Qwen3-8B (75.71) by +5.59 (Ins, 81.30) to +5.39 (DPO, 81.10), and the base RedSage-8B-Base (84.56) beats Qwen3-8B-Base (80.81) by +3.75. On general Open-LLM-Leaderboard tasks, RedSage-8B-DPO reaches 74.33 mean, beating all 8B baselines and even Qwen3-32B (73.17). CFW and Seed give complementary gains (CFW helps SecBench/CyberMetric/MMLU/ARC-C; Seed helps CTI-RCM/MAET/GSM8K). Tool-use is identified as the hardest category in open-ended evaluation. It still trails GPT-5 (86.29 cyber mean, 91.07 general mean).
core_contribution: RedSage is an open, 8B-parameter cybersecurity-specialist assistant LLM plus the full data/pipeline stack used to build and evaluate it. The paper's own four claimed contributions are: (1) CyberFineWeb, an ~11.7B-token cybersecurity continual-pretraining corpus built by fine-tuning a ModernBERT-base classifier to filter FineWeb, plus a curated 28,637-item authoritative seed (RedSage-Seed, ~0.15B tokens) from MITRE ATT&CK/CAPEC/CWE, OWASP, pentest write-ups, and CLI/Kali tool docs; (2) RedSage-Conv, a 266K multi-turn SFT dataset produced by an agentic Planner-Agent/Augmenter-Agent pipeline that turns seed chunks into grounded role-based dialogues; (3) RedSage-Bench, a benchmark of 30,000 MCQs plus 240 open-ended Q&A jointly covering knowledge, offensive skills, and tool proficiency (CLI + Kali) with LLM-as-judge quality scoring; (4) the RedSage-8B model itself (continual pretraining on Qwen3-8B-Base, then SFT + DPO), released open with data and code. The distinguishing claim vs. prior cyber-LLMs (PRIMUS/Foundation-Sec-8B/DeepHat, discussed in Related Work) is combining ALL of large-scale CPT + curated data + agentically-augmented SFT + full openness in one effort.
threat_model: This is a domain-adaptation / benchmarking paper, not an attack-or-defense paper, so it has no adversarial threat model in the classic sense. Its security relevance is threefold and stated in the paper: (a) operational — it targets cybersecurity workflows (threat analysis, incident response, vuln management, pentest/tool use); (b) privacy/confidentiality — the motivation is that proprietary API assistants expose sensitive security data, so an 8B locally-deployable model enables privacy-preserving on-prem inference on consumer GPUs; (c) dual-use — the authors explicitly acknowledge in Discussion/Ethics that embedding offensive-security knowledge carries misuse risk, and release the model 'strictly for research' without real-world SecOps deployment. There is no evaluation of adversarial robustness, jailbreak resistance, or harmful-capability elicitation.
limitations: Author-stated: the LLM-generated-and-verified data pipeline may propagate biases or inaccuracies despite screening, and the model's offensive-security knowledge poses inherent dual-use/misuse risk. Reviewer-noted from the paper's own text: (1) the benchmark (RedSage-Bench) is generated from the SAME RedSage-Seed used to build training data, so despite 0.9-similarity decontamination there is potential home-field advantage on their own benchmark; (2) MCQs and much verification rely on LLM-as-judge/teacher LLMs (Llama-3.3-70B, Qwen2.5-72B) rather than independent human authorship at scale (only 240 open-ended items are human-verified); (3) improvements are modest in absolute terms (a few points) and the gap to GPT-5 remains ~5 points cyber / ~17 points general; (4) DPO on general preference data slightly lowers MCQ accuracy vs the Ins variant; (5) no evaluation of safety, jailbreak resistance, or hallucination/factuality of security advice despite the dual-use framing; (6) CPT used only 5 of 20 corpus chunks due to compute, so the corpus is not fully exploited.

datasets_used: CyberFineWeb (own, ~11.7B tokens CPT corpus filtered from FineWeb)
Open LLM Leaderboard: ARC-Challenge, HellaSwag, TruthfulQA, MMLU, WinoGrande, GSM8K, IFEval
quantitative_results: RedSage-Bench MCQ macro-acc: RedSage-8B-Ins 85.73 vs Qwen3-8B 81.85 (+3.88); RedSage-8B-DPO 84.83 (+2.98); RedSage-8B-Ins > Qwen3-32B 85.40; GPT-5 88.68
RedSage-Bench MCQ base: RedSage-8B-Seed 85.21 (best base) vs Qwen3-8B-Base 84.24

Cybersecurity benchmarks mean (Tab 5): RedSage-8B-Base 84.56 vs Qwen3-8B-Base 80.81 (+3.75); RedSage-8B-Ins 81.30 and RedSage-8B-DPO 81.10 vs Qwen3-8B 75.71 (+5.59 / +5.39); Qwen3-32B 82.31; GPT-5 86.29
Open LLM Leaderboard mean (Tab 6): RedSage-8B-DPO 74.33 (best 8B), RedSage-8B-Ins 73.34, vs Qwen3-8B 65.92 and Foundation-Sec-8B-Instruct 69.28; beats Qwen3-32B 73.17; GPT-5 91.07
Open-ended QA: RedSage-8B-DPO best, +7% absolute mean correctness and +0.07 mean quality over second-best Qwen3-8B; RedSage-8B-Ins quality 6.43
Decontamination removed 2.96% of data relative to benchmark size (0.31% of full training corpus)
Augmentation expanded seed by 9.2x samples and 2.3x tokens (28,637 seed -> ~266K conversations)
Training compute: 32xA100-64GB GPUs, global batch 1024, LR 2.5e-6, CPT 1 epoch / SFT 2 epochs
